passages.title: Tea and Industrial Revolution
passages.source_html:
Tea and Industrial Revolution


A 


Alan Macfarlane thinks he could rewrite history. The professor of anthropological science at King's College, Cambridge has, like other historians, spent decades trying to understand the enigma of the Industrial Revolution. Why did this particular important event – the world-changing birth of industry – happen in Britain? And why did it happen at the end of the 18th century?


B 


Macfarlane compares the question to a puzzle. He claims that there were about 20 different factors and all of them needed to be present before the revolution could happen. The chief conditions are to be found in history textbooks. For industry to 'take off', there needed to be the technology and power to drive factories, large urban populations to provide cheap labour, easy transport to move goods around, an affluent middle-class willing to buy mass-produced objects, a market-driven economy, and a political system that allowed this to happen. While this was the case for England, other nations, such as Japan, Holland and France also met some of these criteria. All these factors must have been necessary but not sufficient to cause the revolution. Holland had everything except coal, while China also had many of these factors. Most historians, however, are convinced that one or two missing factors are needed to solve the puzzle.


C 


The missing factors, he proposes, are to be found in every kitchen cupboard. Tea and beer, two of the nation’s favorite drinks, drove the revolution. Tannin, the active ingredient in tea, and hops, used in making beer, both contain antiseptic properties. This – plus the fact that both are made with boiled water – helped prevent epidemics of waterborne diseases, such as dysentery, in densely populated urban areas. The theory initially sounds eccentric but his explanation of the detective work that went into his deduction and the fact his case has been strengthened by a favorable appraisal of his research by Roy Porter (distinguished medical historian) the skepticism gives way to wary admiration.


D 


Historians had noticed one interesting factor around the mid-18th century that required explanation. Between about 1650 and 1740, the population was static. But then there was a burst in population. The infant mortality rate halved in the space of 20 years, and this happened in both rural areas and cities, and across all classes. Four possible causes have been suggested. There could have been a sudden change in the viruses and bacteria present at that time, but this is unlikely. Was there a revolution in medical science? But this was a century before Lister introduced antiseptic surgery. Was there a change in environmental conditions? There were improvements in agriculture that wiped out malaria, but these were small gains. Sanitation did not become widespread until the 19th century. The only option left was food. But the height and weight statistics show a decline. So the food got worse. Efforts to explain this sudden reduction in child deaths appeared to draw a blank.


E 


This population burst seemed to happen at just the right time to provide labor for the Industrial Revolution. But why? When the Industrial Revolution started, it was economically efficient to have people crowded together forming towns and cities. But with crowded living conditions comes disease, particularly from human waste. Some research in the historical records revealed that there was a change in the incidence of waterborne disease at that time, the English were protected by the strong antibacterial agent in hops, which were added to make beer last. But in the late 17th century a tax was introduced on malt. The poor turned to water and gin, and in the 1720s the mortality rate began to rise again.


F 


Macfarlane looked to Japan, which was also developing large cities about the same time, and also had no sanitation. Waterborne diseases in the Japanese population were far fewer than those in Britain. Could it be the prevalence of tea in their culture? That was when Macfarlane thought about the role of tea in Britain. The history of tea in Britain provided an extraordinary coincidence of dates. Tea was relatively expensive until Britain started direct hade with China in the early 18th century. By the 1740s, about the time that infant mortality was falling, the drink was common. Macfarlane guesses that the fact that water had to be boiled, together with the stomach-purifying properties of tea so eloquently described in Buddhist texts, meant that the breast milk provided by mothers was healthier than it had ever been. No other European nation drank tea so often as the British, which, by Macfarlane's logic, pushed the other nations out of the race for the Industrial Revolution.


G 


But, if tea is a factor in the puzzle, why didn’t this cause an industrial revolution in Japan? Macfarlane notes that in the 17th century, Japan had large cities, high literacy rates and even a futures market. However, Japan decided against a work-based revolution, by giving up labor-saving devices even animals, to avoid putting people out of work. Astonishingly, the nation that we now think of as one of the most technologically advanced, entered the 19th century having almost abandoned the wheel. While Britain was undergoing the Industrial Revolution, Macfarlane notes wryly, Japan was undergoing an industrious one.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1-7


The passage has seven sections, A-G. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph and move it into the gap.


Options (Word Bank):
A. Cases of Japan, Holland and France
B. City development in Japan
C. Tea drinking in Japan and Britain
D. Failed to find a plausible cause for mystery about lower mortality rate
E. Preconditions necessary for industrial revolution
F. Time and place of industrialization
G. Conclusion drawn from the comparison with Japan
H. Relation between population and changes of drink in Britain
I. Two possible solutions to the puzzle
1
正确答案： 1 F. Time and place of industrialization  |  2 E. Preconditions necessary for industrial revolution  |  3 I. Two possible solutions to the puzzle  |  4 D. Failed to find a plausible cause for mystery about lower mortality rate  |  5 H. Relation between population and changes of drink in Britain  |  6 C. Tea drinking in Japan and Britain  |  7 G. Conclusion drawn from the comparison with Japan
Question 1

原文答案句：The professor of anthropological science at King’s College, Cambridge has, like other historians, spent decades trying to understand the enigma of the Industrial Revolution. Why did this particular important event – the world-changing birth of industry – happen in Britain? And why did it happen at the end of the 18th century?

对应段落：A段

审题：题目要求概括A段的主要内容，即讨论工业革命发生的时间和地点。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"Time and place of industrialization"定位到A段中关于工业革命发生的时间和地点的描述。

解题思路：A段主要讲述了Alan Macfarlane教授试图理解工业革命这个谜题，并提出了工业革命发生在英国以及18世纪末的原因。这与选项F"Time and place of industrialization"（工业革命的时间和地点）完全吻合。


Question 2

原文答案句：The chief conditions are to be found in history textbooks. For industry to ‘take off’, there needed to be the technology and power to drive factories, large urban populations to provide cheap labour easy transport to move goods around, an affluent middle-class willing to buy mass-produced objects, a market-driven economy, and a political system that allowed this to happen.

对应段落：B段

审题：题目要求为B段选择一个合适的标题。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"B Paragraph"可以定位到文章的B段。

解题思路：B段主要讲述了工业革命发生所需的条件，包括技术、能源、劳动力、交通、市场和政治体系等。这些条件是工业革命发生的必要因素，但并非充分条件。因此，B段的主题是工业革命发生的条件。根据选项，E选项"Preconditions necessary for the Industrial Revolution"与B段内容相符，因此选择E作为B段的标题。


Question 3

原文答案句：The missing factors, he proposes, are to be found in every kitchen cupboard. Tea and beer, two of the nation’s favorite drinks, drove the revolution.

对应段落：C段  

审题：题目要求为C段选择一个合适的标题，概括该段的主要内容。  

定位关键信息：通过题目中的段落编号"C"定位到C段，寻找该段的核心观点或主题句。  

解题思路：C段的核心内容是Alan Macfarlane提出茶和啤酒是推动工业革命的关键因素，因为它们含有抗菌成分且使用沸水制作，有助于预防水传播疾病。这一观点是该段的主旨，因此选择与"茶和啤酒推动革命"相关的标题（选项I）。


Question 4

原文答案句：Historians had noticed one interesting factor around the mid-18th century that required explanation. Between about 1650 and 1740, the population was static. But then there was a burst in population. The infant mortality rate halved in the space of 20 years, and this happened in both rural areas and cities, and across all classes....Efforts to explain this sudden reduction in child deaths appeared to draw a blank.

对应段落：D段  

审题：题目要求为D段选择一个合适的标题，概括该段的主要内容。  

定位关键信息：通过阅读D段，可以发现该段主要讨论了18世纪中期人口变化的现象，特别是婴儿死亡率下降和人口激增的情况，提出了可能的解释，最后发现找不到死亡率下降的主要原因。  

解题思路：D段的核心内容是关于18世纪中期人口变化的现象及其可能的原因，这与选项D“没有找到死亡率下降的合理原因”高度吻合。原文中明确提到了人口在1650年至1740年间保持稳定，之后突然激增，婴儿死亡率在20年内减半，这一现象无法解释，因此D是最合适的标题。


Question 5

原文答案句： Some research in the historical records revealed that there was a change in the incidence of waterborne disease at that time, the English were protected by the strong antibacterial agent in hops, which were added to make beer last. But in the late 17th century a tax was introduced on malt. The poor turned to water and gin, and in the 1720s the mortality rate began to rise again.

对应段落：E段

审题：题目要求为E段选择一个合适的标题。

定位关键信息：通过阅读E段内容，找到该段的主题句或关键信息，即人口激增为工业革命提供了劳动力，以及拥挤的居住条件带来的疾病问题。

解题思路：E段主要讨论了人口激增为工业革命提供劳动力的同时，也带来了疾病问题，水传播疾病发病率出现波动。段落中还提到了啤酒中的抗菌剂对英国人的保护作用，以及税收政策对死亡率的间接影响。因此，E段的主题是关于工业革命时期的劳动力、疾病和啤酒之间的关系。根据选项内容，选项H“Relation between population and changes of drink in Britain”最符合E段的主题。


Question 6

原文答案句：Macfarlane looked to Japan, which was also developing large cities about the same time, and also had no sanitation. Waterborne diseases in the Japanese population were far fewer than those in Britain. Could it be the prevalence of tea in their culture? That was when Macfarlane thought about the role of tea in Britain.

对应段落：F段

审题：题目要求为F段选择一个合适的标题。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的段落编号F定位到文章的F段，阅读该段内容，找出能够概括段落主旨的关键信息。

解题思路：F段主要讲述了Macfarlane对比日本和英国的情况，发现日本在没有卫生设施的情况下，水传疾病却比英国少，推测可能与茶文化有关。随后，Macfarlane开始思考茶在英国的作用。因此，F段的主要内容是关于茶在日本和英国的比较，以及茶在英国的作用。根据这一内容，选择与茶在日本和英国比较相关的标题，即C。


Question 7

原文答案句：Macfarlane notes that in the 17th century, Japan had large cities, high literacy rates and even a futures market. However, Japan decided against a work-based revolution, by giving up labor-saving devices even animals, to avoid putting people out of work....While Britain was undergoing the Industrial Revolution, Macfarlane notes wryly, Japan was undergoing an industrious one.

对应段落：G段

审题：题目要求为G段选择一个最合适的标题。

定位关键信息：通过阅读G段内容，找出段落的核心主题和关键信息。

解题思路：G段主要讨论了日本虽然饮茶，但没有发生工业革命的原因。段中提到日本在17世纪有大型城市、高识字率，甚至有期货市场，但日本选择不进行基于劳动的革命，放弃节省劳动力的设备甚至动物，以避免失业。作者指出，英国进行工业革命时，日本正在进行的是“勤劳革命”。因此，G段的核心内容是日本没有发生工业革命的原因，最合适的标题应与此相关。根据选项，G项"Conclusion drawn from the comparison with Japan"最符合G段的主题。


Questions 8-13 


Do the following statements agree with the information given in the reading passage? In boxes 8-13 on your answer sheet, write TRUE if the statement is true FALSE if the statement is false NOT GIVEN if the information is not given in the passage


8

The industrialization did not happen in China because of its inefficient railway transportation.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：All these factors must have been necessary but not sufficient to cause the revolution. Holland had everything except coal, while China also had many of these factors.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是“工业革命没有在中国发生是因为其低效的铁路运输”这一陈述是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词“China”和“railway transportation”在文章中定位，文章中只是简要带过，说明中国也具有工业革命的部分条件，但文章中并未提及中国铁路运输效率问题。

解题思路：根据文章内容，中国没有被提及与铁路运输相关的原因，文章也没有说明工业革命没有在中国发生的原因。因此，题目中的信息在文章中未给出。


9

Tea and beer contributed to protect people from waterborne disease.

正确答案： A. TRUE

原文答案句：Tea and beer, two of the nation’s favorite drinks, drove the revolution. Tannin, the active ingredient in tea, and hops, used in making beer, both contain antiseptic properties. This – plus the fact that both are made with boiled water – helped prevent epidemics of waterborne diseases, such as dysentery, in densely populated urban areas.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问茶和啤酒是否有助于保护人们免受水传播疾病的侵害。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的"Tea and beer"和"protect people from waterborne disease"定位到C段，找到关于茶和啤酒的描述。

解题思路：原文明确提到茶和啤酒中的活性成分（茶中的丹宁和啤酒中的啤酒花）具有抗菌特性，加上两者都需要煮沸水制作，这有助于防止水传播疾病（如痢疾）的爆发。因此，题目陈述与原文信息一致，答案为TRUE。


10

Roy Porter disagreed with the proposed theory about the missing factors.

正确答案： B. FALSE

原文答案句：The theory initially sounds eccentric but his explanation of the detective work that went into his deduction and the fact his case has been strengthened by a favorable appraisal of his research by Roy Porter (distinguished medical historian) the skepticism gives way to wary admiration.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问的是“Roy Porter不认同这一理论”这一陈述是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"Roy Porter"和"disagreed with the proposed theory"来定位答案在文章中的位置。

解题思路：在C段中，原文提到“his case has been strengthened by a favorable appraisal of his research by Roy Porter”，意思是Roy Porter对Alan Macfarlane的研究给予了积极的评价，这表明Roy Porter是同意该理论的，而不是不同意。因此，题目中的陈述与文章信息不符，应选择False。


11

The reason of lower child deaths is fully explained by food.

正确答案： B. FALSE

原文答案句：The only option left was food. But the height and weight statistics show a decline. So the food got worse. Efforts to explain this sudden reduction in child deaths appeared to draw a blank.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是“儿童死亡率降低的原因，是否可以完全用食物解释”。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的“lower child deaths”和“food”定位到D段。

解题思路：原文提到食物是解释儿童死亡率降低的唯一选项，但随后指出体重和身高的下降了，说明食物质量下降了，并且解释儿童死亡率突然降低的努力似乎没有结果（draw a blank），这意味着食物并不是完全解释儿童死亡率降低的原因。因此，题干中的“fully explained”与原文信息不符，答案为False。


12

The British made beer by themselves.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：Tannin, the active ingredient in tea, and hops, used in making beer, both contain antiseptic properties. This – plus the fact that both are made with boiled water – helped prevent epidemics of waterborne diseases, such as dysentery, in densely populated urban areas.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问“英国人自己酿造啤酒”，需要判断文章是否提供了相关信息。

定位关键信息：根据题目中的关键词"British"和"made beer"，在文章中定位到C段，该段提到"beer, two of the nation’s favorite drinks"，并提到"hops, used in making beer"。

解题思路：C段提到啤酒是英国人的饮品之一，并说明了啤酒中含有抗菌成分，但没有明确说明啤酒是由英国人自己酿造的，只是提到了啤酒在英国的流行。因此，文章没有提供英国人自己酿造啤酒的具体信息，答案应为NOT GIVEN。


13

Tax on malt indirectly affected the increase of population in late 17th century.

正确答案： A. TRUE

原文答案句：But in the late 17th century a tax was introduced on malt. The poor turned to water and gin, and in the 1720s the mortality rate began to rise again. 

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问“麦芽税是否间接影响了17世纪晚期的人口增长”，需要判断文章是否提供了相关信息。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的"Tax on malt"和"time"定位到E段，原文提到工业革命时期正值人口激增，密集的人口也带来了传染病，而啤酒中的啤酒花带有抗菌成分，降低了水传播疾病发病率，但17世纪后期，麦芽税使得穷人喝不起啤酒，只能饮用水和杜松子酒，死亡率再次攀升。

解题思路：原文中明确提到人口在1650-1740年间先稳定后增长。研究表明是因为人们饮用啤酒，而啤酒中的啤酒花带有抗菌成分，降低了水传播疾病发病率。又提到麦芽税让穷人负担不起啤酒，导致死亡率上升，影响人口增长。因此，题目中所述信息与原文一致。
